speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 28, 1997. We are with Kaethe Wells, maiden name Schohl. The interviewer is Judith Richardson Haimes. We are in Tampa, Florida in the United States of America. We will be conducting this interview in English. Today is February 28, 1997. We are with Kaethe Wells Schohl. I'm Judith Richardson Haimes. We are in Tampa, Florida in the United States of America. We will be conducting this interview in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by. And any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please give me your full name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaethe, K-A-E-T-H-E, Wells, W-E-L-L-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schohl. S-C-H-O-H-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever use any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And may I have your birth date please?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 73 years young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city were you born, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Germany in a village called Florsheim am Main, F-L-O-umlaut-R-S-H-E-I-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, for the first years of your life, what kind of life you lived in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, we had, really, a wonderful life. I have a sister, an older sister. And my father was a chemist. And the factory was sort of catty corner across the street. And we had a three-story house. It was a very beautiful life before Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what kind of a house? Was it a single dwelling or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a single-- it was a family house with so many bedrooms, bathroom, maid's room on the third floor, full basement. When I see the houses today, I think of-- there was the wine cellar. There was the cellar for the potatoes. There was-- there was no refrigerator in those days. There was a chamber. It was called the cooling chamber, where you kept butter and eggs and milk. And you grew up with a nurse. And then as you began to walk, came the nursemaid. And mother was a wonderful housewife. She lived for her husband and her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father did not-- may I explain? Because my grandmother was not only orthodox, but she kept kosher. And in the kitchen was a special cupboard that was locked. And when grandmother came, she unlocked it, her dishes. My father went through World War I. And when he came back, he said, no, we are not keeping kosher. What all I ate during the war-- he just simply did not believe in it. He was a scientist, and he thought different than we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider your family a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were religious. We were very Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go off into shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the high holidays, but the village synagogue did not have a rabbi. My father was president of the-- that little synagogue. On the high holidays, we imported a rabbi from the next larger town. But then in 1934, when we went to Jewish schools, we went every week, every Saturday morning, we attended synagogue in Mainz, M-A-I-N-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a sister. Was she an older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she's older.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of things-- let's say up until 1934-- what kind of things did you do for play and recreation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sort of a tomboy. We swam in summer, hiked, bicycle rides. On weekends, we went-- my father owned a-- what is the word for a property with a sweet water, with sweet water. We went fishing. And in winter, in the mountains, we went hunting. But most-- it was a sport. Mostly, it was a sport, not so much to kill animals and to catch fish. It was a sport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up until, say, 1934, did you consider your childhood a good childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea of what you wanted to be when you were a grown up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that point, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a mostly Jewish neighborhood or was it a mixed neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, until we attended the Jewish school, we had no Jewish friends. All our friends were German Christian. There was actually no difference made what religion, because until Hitler came, being Jewish was a religion. He said it was a race. OK. Our Christian neighbors-- on the high holidays, we walked to the synagogue, and they would say happy new year, doctor. And it was a very friendly village. That's why it was so hard, what came afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, say, up until 1934, where would you consider your family financially? Were they average? Were they better off than the average person?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very wealthy man. He was a-- a chemist, a physicist, scientist, and owned a chemical plant, a little competition with the big IG Farben in Hoechst. And Mother had two maids. And she never handled money. Neither did my father. And a chauffeur would take her wherever she wanted to go. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the area that you lived, were there other wealthy people or were you, say, the most wealthy in the neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- Yes, there were wealthy people. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first become aware of any kind of changes of atmosphere?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- on Saturday afternoons, my father would be at home. And usually the family gathered in the living room. And he wanted to instill in us the beauty of music. And so we would listen to opera, sometimes operettas, but mostly opera. And he would explain what it was. And when we were older, he would take us to the opera, and-- or we would listen to the radio. And I heard my parents talk about Hindenburg being-- he was the president then-- being so ill and that little upstart Hitler. And my father would say, he'll never make it. Never. And that's first when I saw the worry in my parents' voice and faces.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old were you then?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any incidents that made you become aware of the differences between the Jews and the Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went-- the fifth grade, I left the elementary school in Florsheim and went to Russelsheim, what we call a gymnasium, which is equivalent to a high school. And at first, everything was fine. And then after Hitler came to power, I could tell that there was a sudden coldness in my classroom. There was only one girl who would still talk to me. The others just sort of-- and my sister felt the same way. I wasn't very happy then. There was in that whole school-- it was called the [GERMAN]-- that-- that whole school, I believe there were only two other Jewish children. Yes. No, we weren't very happy then. But that was the only school around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any changes that you recognized? Say, like in 1932, you were a very happy child. And how about in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you are young, and we didn't understand too much about it. All we knew, we saw that our parents were worried, so that made us unhappy at times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were children, and it didn't last.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about 1934?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father came home one time and said that we can't go back to school anymore, all Jewish children. That was it. Practically overnight, all over Germany, Jewish schools came up. And what I'm sure the Nazi regime did not know-- had they known, they would have never done it-- the Jewish children got the finest education anybody could ever ask, because our teachers were professors from the-- Jewish professors from the university. Our chemistry teacher was a professor, Dr. Manheimer. We had the finest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was. You were-- you weren't hated any more. You were among your own. Yes, it was. I would say we had a happy childhood, even then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said your father was a chemist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me for whom did he work?

speaker: interviewee
text: For himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did his business have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German it was called [GERMAN], chemical factory Electro was the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of chemicals did they manufacture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly dyes for leather. But they also-- they also made soap. And I remember, there was a carpenter shop. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. That's really all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he employ many people?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were Jews or Gentiles? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only Jewish person I remember at that time was his cousin, who was his private secretary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the townspeople--

speaker: interviewee
text: Work there, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: --like your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very much. I was too young to remember that. But I have read it in speeches and in newspaper clippings that-- they named a street after my father in 1984. Yes. And so I-- I read it there. During the Depression-- I guess that was all over the world in the late 1920s-- my father hired two women and rented a building. And there was a soup kitchen that went day and night for anybody who needed it. He did-- he did so much that one newspaper article said that the neighborhood children who were so poor, before they went to school in the mornings, they stopped at the Schohl house to get-- pick up sandwiches to take to school with them. And at Christmastime, my mother and the two maids fixed baskets upon baskets. And one time, she took me. I forgot. I must have been seven or eight years old. It was horrible the way some people lived. And-- but my mother wanted me to see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did your father keep his factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I can't remember if it was '35 or '36. I think it was '36 that they took the factory away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the government. No Jewish person was allowed to own a factory. And so two party members, Nazi Party members, were set on top of the factory. The factory went on without my father for a while. And then it was moved to another city, because they detonated everything. They needed that property to build houses. And today, there are houses. But the [GERMAN] goes through that today.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father continue to make a living after his factory was gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government froze everything we had. Our maids were not allowed to work in Jewish homes anymore, so we were alone. The car, of course, was taken away. Everything was taken away. The government gave us so much money each month from our own money. And it was too much to die, too little to live on. He got on a bicycle and rode to former customers, clients, and friends and did a little business on the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the factory was taken away from your family, how did the-- the people in your village and the people who had worked for your father treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all very, very sorry. As a matter of fact, how did we live, you said. Until 1940, people, the grocer, the butcher, the baker came at night. Nobody, of course, was allowed-- was afraid to come in a Jewish home during the day. They came at night with baskets of food. And my mother would always say, I can't pay you. We don't want-- we just want you to know we have nothing to do with it. It's the Nazis. We are not Nazis. We're not-- that's, that's, that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents tell you and your sister in the home about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew what was going on. I mean, we, we, we talked about it very-- yes, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, as long as we're together, we did not-- none of us seemed to be afraid. My father never showed any fear. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you or did you ever, while still living there in your home, experience any acts of anti-Semitism from any of your neighbors and friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're getting to 1938 and the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not-- we still went to school in Mainz. We took the train to Mainz in the morning and the train back in the afternoon. And November 9, I believe, early in the morning, the radio came through that a Jewish boy killed a German attache in France. And they said that in the big cities, the people rose and struck out against all the Jews because they were so furious about what that young Jewish boy did. And they destroyed all stores they could get their hands on and et cetera. It's been talked so much about it. At that time, my sister and I were going to Frankfurt on the train. She went to a school to learn how to sew, and, and I went to a school to become a cosmetic-- I learned how to become a masseuse, do cosmetics. I wish I [LAUGHS] could tell you that in English, but I can't right now. It was a salon for cosmetics. And I learned how to do this after school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We said we didn't want to go. And my father said, nobody's going to touch us. You know, some things you just don't forget. And my mother said, let them, you know, go ahead and go. My sis and I took the train. We felt very uneasy. We arrived in Frankfurt and had to walk maybe two blocks. And on one of the main streets, we saw where the Jewish stores used to be. The streets were littered, littered with all the merchandise. And I said, let's go back home. And we did. We turned around and took the next train home and told what we saw. And then, little by little, the stories came in. In that town, they destroyed, in that town, they destroyed-- and my father kept saying, not here. There are only 14 Jewish families. They are all so well-liked. Nobody's going to touch us. We ate a very uneasy dinner that night. And the four of us sat in the living room, very quiet at the-- and it was very quiet outside. And my sister finally said she was going to bed. She went upstairs. And at 9:00 o'clock, my father said, I told you, nothing is going to happen. I'm going to sleep. He went upstairs. And my mother and I sat in the living room, and I looked at the-- for the last time-- at a huge grandfather clock, and it struck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that moment, it sounded like a bomb fell on the house. There was such a terrific noise. And of course, now I know what it was. We had iron shades on the outside of every window. There were 30 SR. Do you know what SR? But not in uniform. They all wore civilian clothes. But we knew them. How did you know what they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some of them were from the village. And the rest were-- were strangers, but when we saw the ones from the-- of course, we knew then. They had axes. And again, it was my mother who said afterwards, these axes had brand new handles, so it was prepared. Because Dr. Goebbels got on the radio the next day and said this was totally unprepared. We had nothing to do with it. The people did it. And my mother said, uh uh, brand new handles for that. Anyhow, they-- they actually hit through those iron shades everywhere. And then they broke down huge wooden front doors. And they came in. And my father came downstairs. He had put on pants over his nightshirt. And somehow, I can see ourselves, the three of us, standing in the hallway against the wall. I stood on the first step upstairs, then my mother, then my father. And they came by us and started in the kitchen. Everything was demolished, broken with the axes. There was no window left. And then we heard the voice, kill that dirty Jew. And my mother stuck out-- got in front of my father and said, you have to kill me first. And one of them grabbed her here and threw her against the wall. And my father, his face was so white, I don't-- I didn't think that he had any blood left. He said to me-- oh, my grandmother was upstairs, in the meantime. My father's mother couldn't live alone anymore, and she's had a stroke. And thank god she couldn't remember too well. But she was-- and she was bedfast. So my father said, go to grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran upstairs, and I went in her room. And she said, what is it? And I said, it's the Nazis, Oma. And I heard them come upstairs. And I heard my mother scream, go ahead and break everything in the bathroom. We'll all blow up. It was one of those old-fashioned-- so they didn't touch the bathroom. But the beds went out on the street, everything. And I thought, what am I going to-- so I opened the door, and there was this great big guy whom I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, look, my grandmother, she is very, very sick. Please don't come in. He was actually, I think, ashamed, because he had a very peculiar look on his face. And he said, nah, and closed the door. And I went back to my grandmother. And then you heard a whistle. And within seconds, 30 men-- because our neighbor counted them-- were gone. They had-- they came in trucks. But they came without lights, and it made no noise. And they got on the trucks and left. We looked out the broken window. The street was black with people. But you could have heard a needle drop. No one made one sound. And then my sister was downstairs, too, and my mother had a stroke. So my father said, let's make a bed for her upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had a stroke at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time. My-- we didn't know that. She fell down. And my father said, let's take her upstairs, make a bed on the floor. We grabbed what we still had and made a bed for her on the floor. And she said, my pills. She had a bad heart. Her pills had to be taken with milk. When I went in the kitchen, there was nothing. I mean, the food that was there was full of glass and-- so I said to my father, what am I going to do? He said, climb over the wall to our-- the Hoffmans, our neighbors, wonderful neighbors, and get milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And that's what I did. And climbed back again, and so she could take her pills. And these Hoffmans, these people, from then on, they came every night over the wall and brought us. And they were crying and carrying on. What could they do? To this day, they are the only Germans that I correspond with, to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in your home after that incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that incident, the next morning, there was another incident, and that's when my father was taken to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll continue this on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are we off?
